summary: SKGLView render thread silently stops after 8+ hours of continuous background rendering on UWP (DrawInBackground=true) — likely caused by unhandled EGL/ANGLE SwapBuffers failure with no device-lost recovery in AngleSwapChainPanel.RenderFrame.

stepsToReproduce: Create a Xamarin.Forms UWP app using SKGLView with a custom renderer that sets DrawInBackground=true
Render streaming signal data continuously using the render loop
Let the application run for more than 8 hours
Observe that the draw callback stops being invoked — no exception is surfaced
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 2.88.0-preview.178, Visual Studio 2019, Windows 10 v1809 (Target: 10.0.17763.0), UWP platform

bugSignals.errorMessage: Render thread stops silently — draw callback is no longer invoked after 8+ hours

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The AngleSwapChainPanel.RenderFrame() method still contains the same empty SwapBuffers failure recovery block in the current codebase — the TODO comment has never been implemented.

analysis.summary: The render thread silently stops after prolonged background rendering because AngleSwapChainPanel.RenderFrame() has no recovery logic when glesContext.SwapBuffers() fails (e.g., due to D3D/ANGLE device lost). The failure block at line 233 is empty with a TODO comment. After a SwapBuffers failure, subsequent RenderFrame() calls either: (a) early-return because the surface state becomes invalid, causing the render loop to spin doing nothing, or (b) throw in MakeCurrent() which kills the background thread silently. Neither path recovers the EGL context or surfaces the error.
analysis.rationale: This is a genuine bug — the code has an explicit TODO for device-lost recovery that was never implemented. The reporter's hypothesis about SwapBuffers failure is well-reasoned and supported by the source code. The issue is specific to UWP/WinUI ANGLE-based rendering via AngleSwapChainPanel. The 8+ hour timeframe is consistent with D3D device lost events (GPU timeout detection, driver reset, or resource exhaustion over time). Related issue #1490 shows similar DrawInBackground+UWP instability (AccessViolationException on navigation).

keySignals.text: when the application runs for a very long time (more than 8 hours), the render thread simply stops
keySignals.interpretation: Consistent with D3D/ANGLE device-lost event after prolonged GPU usage — the render context becomes invalid.

keySignals.text: No exception is thrown, the draw method is simply not called anymore
keySignals.interpretation: The render loop either spins silently (early return in RenderFrame) or the background thread dies from an unhandled exception that doesn't propagate to UI.

keySignals.text: I am wondering if glesContext.SwapBuffers() might fail at some point
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter identified the exact code path — the empty recovery block in RenderFrame confirms this hypothesis.

keySignals.text: Might investigate what happens if GRContext becomes invalid when drawing with DrawInBackground
keySignals.interpretation: Community confirmation that GRContext invalidation during background drawing is a plausible root cause.

codeInvestigation.finding: SwapBuffers failure recovery block is empty — the comment says 'we must reinitialize EGL and the GL resources' but no recovery code exists. This is the root cause: when SwapBuffers fails after prolonged use (device lost), nothing recovers the context.

RenderFrame() guard: 'if (designMode || !isLoaded || !isVisible || glesContext?.HasSurface != true) return;' — after a device-lost event, if the surface becomes invalid, subsequent calls early-return silently without any logging or notification.

RenderLoop() has no try-catch around RenderFrame(). If MakeCurrent() or any GL call throws after device loss, the exception kills the async thread silently — IAsyncAction transitions to Error status but no one monitors it.
SwapBuffers() returns bool (false on failure) but MakeCurrent() throws Exception on failure. Inconsistent error handling — one path returns silently, the other throws.
OnRenderFrame() creates GRContext lazily but never recreates it after device loss. If the GL context is invalidated, the existing GRContext/surface/renderTarget all reference stale GPU resources.

nextQuestions: Does the issue also affect EnableRenderLoop=true without DrawInBackground? The empty recovery block affects both code paths.
Is the 8-hour timeframe consistent across devices or does it vary with GPU driver / D3D feature level?
Does the WinUI (non-UWP) path using the same AngleSwapChainPanel exhibit the same issue?

resolution.hypothesis: After 8+ hours of continuous rendering, the ANGLE/D3D layer experiences a device-lost event (GPU timeout detection, driver reset, or resource exhaustion). SwapBuffers returns false, but the empty recovery block in RenderFrame means the EGL context and GL resources are never reinitialized. The render loop either spins calling RenderFrame (which early-returns due to invalid surface state) or crashes silently from a MakeCurrent() exception.

proposals.title: Implement SwapBuffers failure recovery
proposals.description: Implement the TODO in AngleSwapChainPanel.RenderFrame() to reinitialize EGL and GL resources when SwapBuffers fails. Destroy the current surface and context, recreate them via GlesContext.Reset() + EnsureRenderSurface(), and invalidate SKSwapChainPanel's GRContext/surface/renderTarget to force recreation on the next OnRenderFrame call.

proposals.title: Add try-catch with recovery in render loop
proposals.description: Wrap RenderFrame() calls in RenderLoop() and RenderOnce() with try-catch. On EGL/GL exceptions, attempt context recovery instead of letting the background thread die. Log the error and attempt reinitializaiton before giving up.

proposals.title: Use CPU-based SKCanvasView instead of SKGLView
proposals.description: As a workaround, switch from SKGLView (GPU/ANGLE-based) to SKCanvasView (CPU raster). This avoids the ANGLE/EGL device-lost issue entirely, at the cost of lower rendering performance. For streaming signal data this may be acceptable if frame rates are modest.

resolution.recommendedReason: The empty recovery block is clearly a TODO that was never completed. The fix aligns with what the comment already describes: reinitialize EGL and GL resources on SwapBuffers failure. This addresses the root cause rather than working around it.

actionability.reason: The empty SwapBuffers recovery block is clearly the root cause, but reproducing and verifying a fix requires long-running UWP testing with device-lost simulation. The code analysis is strong but the 8+ hour repro cycle makes verification expensive.

actions.description: Apply bug, views, and UWP platform labels
Acknowledge the bug and confirm the reporter's hypothesis with code references
actions.comment:
Thanks for the detailed analysis — your hypothesis about `SwapBuffers` failure is correct.

The recovery block in `AngleSwapChainPanel.RenderFrame()` is empty (it has a TODO comment but no implementation). When `eglSwapBuffers` fails after prolonged use (likely a D3D device-lost event), nothing reinitializes the EGL context or GL resources. The render loop then either spins doing nothing (because the surface state check at the top of `RenderFrame` early-returns) or dies silently if `MakeCurrent()` throws on the now-invalid context.

The fix would involve implementing the recovery logic described in that TODO: destroy and recreate the EGL surface/context, then force SKSwapChainPanel to recreate its GRContext and render target.

As a short-term workaround, you could periodically check whether rendering has stalled (e.g., track frame timestamps) and force recreation of the SKGLView when it stops responding. This is heavy-handed but could keep a long-running app alive until a proper fix is in place.

actions.description: Cross-reference with #1490 (AccessViolationException with DrawInBackground on UWP)
